5.1.label: Question 5.1
5.1.scenario:
In a country, the State provides universal non-contributory sickness benefits for those in the workforce who have been unable to work for five or more days as the result of sickness or injury.

The amount of sickness benefit is related to pre-sickness income for the first three months of sickness. After this, it is paid at a flat rate equal to national average earnings.

The Department of Labour, which is responsible for the regulation and administration of the sickness benefit scheme, has provided the following information:

• About 75% of the population of 50m people of working age are in the workforce, ie they have a job or are actively seeking work.
• About 5% of the workforce is unemployed.
• About 6m of the workforce are currently sick, and about half of these are receiving sickness benefits. This is the highest number of claimants in the last 20 years.
• There are substantial differences between the regions of the country in both the proportion of the workforce that is unemployed, and in the proportion that is receiving sickness benefits.
• In the last few years the number of new claimants each year has been falling.
• In the last few years the total number of those receiving sickness benefits at any time has been rising steadily.

(i).synopsis: Low unemployment/involuntary, incidence falling but duration rising, changing morbidity to chronic conditions (back/stress), lax claims control, long-term claimants finding it hard to return to work, regional variation reflects employment patterns
(i).question: Discuss why the country may have this current experience and seen the recent changes.

Reasons for the rapid rise in the number: Only a small proportion of the workforce is unemployed and these are likely to be mainly the workers who are between jobs. [½]
So, overall there is very little involuntary unemployment. There may, however, be regional variations in this pattern. [½]
About half of those who fall sick, and are unable to work, recover within five working days and so receive no sickness benefits. [½]

The incidence rate for sickness has been falling. If this fall had occurred over a much longer period of time, it may be the result of a structural change in the workforce (eg the workforce becoming younger or there being fewer arduous manual jobs). [1]
However, because it has occurred over a shorter period it is probably a genuine fall in the incidence of sickness. [½]
The steady rise in the total numbers receiving benefits must be the result of reduced recovery rates from sickness (or at least a lower rate of return to work so causing benefits to cease). [½]
This could be the result of changing patterns of sickness, ... [½]
... eg more people suffering from chronic conditions (such as back problems or stress) ... [½]
... and fewer people suffering from more acute conditions (such as broken bones or infectious diseases). [½]
This could also be the result of more lax claims control (ie less active monitoring and counselling of those receiving benefit). [½]
Eventually the long-term claimants get used to their 'new way of life' and find the transition back to work very difficult. [½]
Because the change has occurred over a relatively short period of time, a failure of claims control seems to be a more likely explanation than a change in morbidity patterns. [½]
The regional variation in numbers of claimants probably reflects the patterns of employment in each region. Some regions will have little heavy industry and most jobs will be office jobs. [½]
A given level of sickness will result in fewer claimants in this environment than in a region with a greater proportion of manual jobs in factories. [½]

(ii).synopsis: Longer deferred period, upper limit on benefit period, reduce flat rate, tighten definition of unable to work, stricter certification scheme, in-house certification for long-duration claims, counselling/transitional benefits, linked claims period
(ii).question:
The annual expenditure on sickness benefits in this country is at an all-time high. The State is keen to reduce this expenditure.

Describe how the cost of sickness benefits could be reduced.

Reducing the cost of sickness benefits: Make the length of the deferred period greater than five days. [½]
This will reduce the number of claimants and the length of claims. [½]
Consider imposing an upper limit on the period for which benefits can be claimed. [½]
This would reduce the number of long-term claimants, which is the prime cause of the increased costs. [½]
However, this might be seen as unethical because people usually become sick and unable to work through no fault of their own. [½]
Reduce the flat rate paid after three months of sickness. [½]

Paying national average earnings (NAE) may not provide a sufficient incentive for the lower paid to make every effort to return to work. For example, pay a proportion of NAE after three months of sickness. [½]
Whether an individual is entitled to claim or not depends on how 'being unable to work' is defined. [½]
The high level of claims may be the result of a tight definition (eg being unable to do the job you were doing immediately before you fell sick). [½]

While it might be reasonable to maintain this definition for the first few months of sickness, as claimants will want to return to their previous job and lifestyle, the definition could be widened after an initial period. [½]
Many claimants will be capable of earning a living, but perhaps in a less arduous role than before. [½]
For example, someone suffering from long-term stress may not be capable of returning to their original job, but may be physically able to do another, less stressful job. [½]
With an active counselling service, claimants may even be keen to follow this route. This would be effective if the cost of the counselling service was less than the claim costs saved. [½]
Claimants could be offered a reduced benefit for a transitional period, if their new employment was less well paid than their original job. [½]
Claims costs will rise if the criteria for claiming both at the beginning and during a spell of sickness or injury are not enforced correctly. [½]
It may be that the method of certifying eligibility to claim has become less effective because the pattern of morbidity has changed. [½]

There could be more claims for conditions, such as psychiatric illnesses, that require more time and expertise for a reliable diagnosis compared with conditions, such as infectious diseases, that can be easily diagnosed. [½]
Practitioners with little experience or limited time may certify these illnesses much too readily in order to avoid disputes with claimants. [½]
Hence, the cost effectiveness of the certification scheme should be reviewed. [½]
For example, for longer-duration claims, the department might operate its own in-house certification system using full-time occupational health doctors and nurses. [½]
After, say, eight weeks of claim, the claimants would need such certification in order to continue to receive benefits. [½]
Thereafter, full recertification every eight weeks, say, with more-frequent less-stringent checks might be effective. [½]
These measures could be monitored by looking at the changes in claim termination rates resulting from changed certification procedures. [½]
Experimental schemes could be piloted in smaller representative areas to test their effectiveness before being implemented more widely. [½]
Introduce a 'linked claims' period (perhaps in conjunction with a longer deferred period). [½]
A large proportion of the increased cost arises from long-term claimants, and so this would encourage long-term claimants to try working again. [½]
[Maximum 9]
